ai_strategy_lead.name: Gary Carini
ai_strategy_lead.title: Vice Provost for Artificial Intelligence and Institutional Research

documents.title: Baylor in Deeds — Strategic Plan (Human-Technology Interface pillar)
GenAI at Baylor (ITS strategy and tool landscape)
Baylor Generative AI Working Group charter

ai_strategy.summary: Baylor frames AI strategy through its 'Baylor in Deeds' strategic plan, where 'Human-Technology Interface' is one of four foundational pillars. As a private R1 Christian university, Baylor explicitly embraces generative AI as a tool for education, research and operations while insisting that human conscience and moral agency cannot be replicated by AI. Strategy is coordinated by Vice Provost for AI and Institutional Research Gary Carini (role created August 2025), the University AI Committee chaired by Engineering Dean Daniel Pack, and the Baylor Generative AI Working Group under the Office of the Provost.

key_provisions: Any new generative AI platforms or chatbots must be reviewed and approved by BaylorITS and the Office of the Provost before adoption
Use of AI tools in academic programs must be transparent
Humans must retain final decision-making authority for employment, financial, and mission-critical decisions informed by AI
Submitted student work generated by AI is a violation of the Honor Code unless the instructor explicitly permits it
Faculty are expected to provide specific syllabus-level statements defining AI use expectations for each course
Use of non-Baylor-hosted AI systems with sensitive data is discouraged due to privacy concerns
ai_policy.summary: Baylor's policy posture is cautious but not prohibitive: an institution-wide review-and-approval gate for new AI tools (ITS + Provost), a default-off honor code stance on AI-generated student work that faculty can selectively open via explicit syllabus statements, and discouragement of sensitive-data use in non-Baylor-hosted AI. The University AI Committee (12 members, chaired by Engineering Dean Daniel Pack) is the body developing and iterating these policies.

bodies.name: University AI Committee
bodies.scope: Institution-wide policy, guidelines, faculty training, and tool evaluation — chaired by Daniel Pack (Dean, School of Engineering and Computer Science); 12 members appointed by Provost Brickhouse at the July 2024 Board of Regents meeting

bodies.name: Baylor Generative AI Working Group
bodies.scope: 18-member group under the Office of the Provost gathering insights, identifying concerns, and highlighting innovations across humanities, social sciences, education, professional schools, STEM, ITS and libraries

bodies.name: AI Roundtable
bodies.scope: Monthly cross-school faculty roundtable exploring philosophical and ethical dimensions of AI, co-led by Toby Brooks and Gary Carini; includes faculty from Education, Honors College, Arts & Sciences, Hankamer, Engineering/CS, Law, Robbins College, Institute for Faith and Learning, and the Academy for Teaching and Learning

bodies.name: Office of the Vice Provost for AI and Institutional Research
bodies.scope: Established August 2025 with Gary Carini as inaugural Vice Provost; coordinates AI initiatives alongside the University AI Committee and Institutional Research

bodies.name: Academy for Teaching and Learning (ATL) — AI Resources
bodies.scope: Centralized AI pedagogy support: recorded sessions, documents, policies and syllabus templates for faculty

bodies.name: AI and Data Ethics Research Group (Ethics Initiative)
bodies.scope: Cross-disciplinary research group within the Baylor Ethics Initiative, co-led by Pablo Rivas (Computer Science) and Neil Messer (Religion)

governance_structure.summary: Baylor's governance is a layered structure: a formal University AI Committee (policy + approvals), a broader 18-member Generative AI Working Group (sensing + input), an ethics-focused AI Roundtable, and the new Vice Provost for AI role (announced 2025) to coordinate across them. ITS owns the enterprise approval gate for new AI tools. The Ethics Initiative's AI and Data Ethics group and Baylor Responsible AI research unit provide the research-and-ethics backbone.

initiatives.title: Vice Provost for AI and Institutional Research established
initiatives.description: Baylor appointed Gary Carini as the inaugural Vice Provost for Artificial Intelligence and Institutional Research effective August 1, 2025, integrating AI strategy with institutional research and giving the Provost's Office a dedicated senior leader for AI coordination.
initiatives.significance: Creating a vice-provost-level AI role at a private Christian R1 signals that AI governance is being treated as a core strategic function rather than an IT concern.

investment.description: Internal reorganization creating a new vice-provost line combining AI strategy with institutional research

initiatives.policy_implications: Establishes a single executive owner for AI strategy who can coordinate across the AI Committee, Generative AI Working Group, AI Roundtable, ITS and the Ethics Initiative.

initiatives.title: University AI Committee sets AI standards
initiatives.description: Provost Nancy Brickhouse established a 12-member University AI Committee at the July 2024 Board of Regents meeting, chaired by School of Engineering and Computer Science Dean Daniel Pack. The committee is charged with developing policies/guidelines, training faculty, identifying beneficial AI platforms, and establishing long-term technology utilization strategies.
initiatives.significance: A provost-chartered, Engineering-dean-led institutional committee is the primary mechanism defining how generative AI is used across a private R1's academic mission.
initiatives.policy_implications: Centralizes AI policy development and faculty training within a standing committee rather than ad-hoc guidance.

initiatives.title: ITS/Provost review-and-approval gate for new AI tools
initiatives.description: Baylor requires that any new generative AI platforms and chatbots—including those developed by faculty, staff or students—undergo review and approval by BaylorITS and the Office of the Provost before adoption. The university also discourages using non-Baylor-hosted AI systems with sensitive data.
initiatives.significance: A unified ITS+Provost intake process is unusual at private R1s and is a concrete lever for enforcing data-privacy and mission-alignment expectations on AI procurement.

investment.description: Operational workload for ITS and Provost's Office

initiatives.policy_implications: Creates a procurement chokepoint that constrains shadow-AI adoption but may slow faculty experimentation.

initiatives.title: Career Center Copilot AI agents
initiatives.description: Baylor's Career Center built three specialized Microsoft Copilot agents — focused on resume-building, interview preparation, and career discovery — by feeding its own ethical-AI use guides into Copilot. Students sign in with Baylor credentials to interact. Additional agents (Graduate School Planning, Career Communications) are planned, along with a LinkedIn integration to surface AI-focused certificates.
initiatives.significance: A student-services agent rollout built on Microsoft Copilot is a concrete example of bringing agentic AI into scale-constrained advising workflows at a private R1.

investment.description: Internal build on existing Microsoft 365 Copilot entitlement

collaborations.description: Copilot agent platform and LinkedIn integration for certificate surfacing

tools_and_vendors.name: Copilot Agents
tools_and_vendors.role: Agent platform hosting resume, interview, and career-discovery agents

initiatives.policy_implications: Demonstrates an approved pattern for deploying Copilot agents on top of internally authored ethical-use guides.

initiatives.title: Bear-Bot financial aid chatbot (Ocelot)
initiatives.description: Baylor launched 'Bear-Bot,' an Ocelot-powered AI chatbot providing 24/7/365 self-service support for financial aid and 'paying for college' questions.
initiatives.significance: One of the earlier enterprise conversational-AI deployments in Baylor's student-facing stack, addressing FAFSA/financial-aid service load.

investment.description: Vendor subscription to Ocelot chatbot platform

collaborations.description: Ocelot provides the chatbot ML platform and FAFSA content library

initiatives.policy_implications: Represents an approved third-party AI product with student data handling responsibilities.

initiatives.title: Baylor site in NSF-funded Center on Responsible AI and Governance (CRAIG)
initiatives.description: Baylor is a site in the NSF Industry-University Cooperative Research Center CRAIG, alongside Ohio State (lead), Rutgers, and Northeastern. Baylor's site director is Assistant Professor of Computer Science Pablo Rivas. CRAIG is described as the first NSF IUCRC focused specifically on responsible AI, with industry members including Meta, Nationwide, Honda Research, Cisco, Worthington Steel and Bread Financial.
initiatives.significance: Positions Baylor in an NSF-funded national consortium on responsible-AI research, and anchors its ethics-forward identity in federally backed infrastructure.

investment.funding_source: U.S. National Science Foundation (IUCRC program) + industry members
investment.description: NSF IUCRC planning and operational funding plus industry member fees

collaborations.description: Primary federal funder via IUCRC program

initiatives.policy_implications: Gives Baylor faculty and students direct industry/federal exposure on responsible-AI research questions.

initiatives.title: Baylor Symposium on Faith and Culture 2026: Technology and the Human Person in the Age of AI
initiatives.description: The Institute for Faith and Learning convened this flagship symposium February 26-28, 2026 on Baylor's campus with 700+ registered attendees, bringing together theologians, philosophers, scientists, technologists, ethicists, and educators to examine AI from a faith-informed perspective.
initiatives.significance: One of the largest faith-informed AI ethics convenings hosted by a private R1, positioning Baylor as a distinctive voice in the national AI-and-values conversation.

investment.funding_source: Baylor Institute for Faith and Learning
investment.description: Symposium hosting; free for Baylor students, faculty and staff

initiatives.policy_implications: Surfaces ethical, theological, and human-flourishing framings that feed back into Baylor's institutional AI posture.

initiatives.title: Hankamer AI Venture Challenge
initiatives.description: Baylor Hankamer School of Business launched what it describes as the nation's first AI-Entrepreneurship Challenge in 2025. Teams of 1-3 undergraduate students submit AI-driven solutions to consumer problems; finalists pitch live, with $5,000 in cash prizes. More than 60 submissions arrived in the inaugural year. The competition ties to the 'Human-Technology Interface' pillar of the Baylor in Deeds strategic plan and was championed by Dean David Szymanski.
initiatives.significance: A university-branded AI startup competition signals embedding AI in the business-school experience and connecting student innovation to the strategic plan.

investment.funding_source: Hankamer School of Business / Baugh Center

initiatives.policy_implications: Frames AI fluency as a business-school expectation and links it to entrepreneurial outcomes.

initiatives.title: JPRNM AI-integrated curriculum and new courses
initiatives.description: The Department of Journalism, Public Relations and New Media has been embedding AI into its curriculum since AY 2023-24. Five instructors (Alec Tefertiller, Rosalynn Vasquez, Elizabeth Bates, Kim Patterson, plus an industry-hire alum) produced a teaching brief and new AI-related courses debuting in AY 2025-26 focused on ethical, industry-relevant AI use in newsrooms, advertising and PR.
initiatives.significance: A department-level, faculty-driven AI curriculum overhaul at a professional-facing program provides a replicable model inside the College of Arts & Sciences.

investment.funding_source: Baylor College of Arts & Sciences / JPRNM
investment.description: Faculty time for course design and teaching brief

collaborations.description: Former faculty member bringing industry perspective

initiatives.policy_implications: Shows how department-level curricular policy coexists with Baylor's default-off honor code stance.

initiatives.title: Library AI research assistant evaluations (Primo OneSearch, Scopus AI, Web of Science Research Assistant)
initiatives.description: Baylor Libraries integrated the Ex Libris Primo Research Assistant into its OneSearch discovery interface and is running parallel campus evaluations of Elsevier's Scopus AI and Clarivate's Web of Science Research Assistant, with a plan to subscribe to one based on user feedback.
initiatives.significance: Library-led vendor evaluation of the major AI-enabled research discovery tools gives Baylor a controlled pathway for introducing generative search to researchers.

investment.description: Subscription/trial fees for Scopus AI and Web of Science Research Assistant; Primo upgrade

initiatives.policy_implications: Evaluation rather than immediate deployment aligns with Baylor's ITS/Provost approval posture.

initiatives.title: Microsoft Copilot in M365 — licensing transition
initiatives.description: Baylor previously offered Copilot Chat directly inside Word, Excel, PowerPoint and OneNote; as of April 15, 2026 Microsoft's new enterprise licensing removed that in-app experience and Baylor is re-evaluating Copilot offerings with the University's budget office.
initiatives.significance: An institutional re-evaluation of paid Copilot entitlements surfaces the tension between desirable in-app AI and licensing costs, a challenge shared across the sector.

investment.description: Ongoing Microsoft 365 + Copilot entitlements under evaluation

collaborations.description: Enterprise Microsoft 365 license with evolving Copilot tiers

tools_and_vendors.name: Microsoft 365 Copilot (Chat)
tools_and_vendors.role: Previously embedded in Word/Excel/PowerPoint/OneNote; under reevaluation

initiatives.policy_implications: Demonstrates the operational reality of depending on vendor pricing moves for campus AI availability.

initiatives.title: Kodiak HPC cluster — GPU nodes for AI research
initiatives.description: Baylor operates the centralized Kodiak HPC cluster with ~100 compute nodes, 9 GPU nodes (NVIDIA GPU systems plus Intel Phi and HP Moonshot), one large-memory system and 2.5PB multi-tiered storage. A condominium model lets PIs expand the cluster.
initiatives.significance: Dedicated GPU capacity (modest by HPC standards but substantial for a private R1) underpins Baylor's AI/ML research footprint.

investment.description: Multi-million-dollar HPC infrastructure including Cray CS400 heritage

tools_and_vendors.name: Kodiak HPC cluster
tools_and_vendors.role: Centralized HPC with GPU nodes for ML training and scientific computing

initiatives.title: AI and Data Ethics Research Group (Ethics Initiative)
initiatives.description: The Baylor Ethics Initiative hosts a standing AI and Data Ethics research group co-led by Pablo Rivas (Computer Science) and Neil Messer (Religion), bringing together affiliated faculty across disciplines to examine how data-driven AI affects society.
initiatives.significance: Institutionalizes cross-disciplinary AI-ethics research within a Christian-missioned research initiative rather than only within Computer Science.
initiatives.policy_implications: Feeds philosophical and theological perspectives into Baylor's institutional AI posture.

initiatives.title: AI & Ethics professional education course series
initiatives.description: Baylor Extended Learning / Graduate Professional Education launched an AI & Ethics course series covering AI applications and ethical considerations, with initial offerings focused on law, healthcare and business audiences.
initiatives.significance: Extends Baylor's AI-ethics positioning to working professionals beyond the degree-seeking population.

investment.funding_source: Baylor Graduate Professional Education / Extended Learning

partners: Regional professionals in law, healthcare, business

initiatives.policy_implications: Packages Baylor's AI-ethics expertise as a revenue-positive non-degree offering.

initiatives.title: Arts & Sciences Strategic Plan — Human-Technology Interface execution
initiatives.description: The College of Arts & Sciences published a strategic plan (2025) aligning with 'Baylor in Deeds' that highlights the Human-Technology Interface as a pillar, including commitments to AI integration in teaching and research across humanities, sciences and social sciences.
initiatives.significance: Translates an institution-wide strategic pillar into college-level commitments and programs, shaping AI adoption across Baylor's largest academic unit.

collaborations.description: Internal coordination around Baylor in Deeds

initiatives.policy_implications: Embeds AI strategy into the largest college's operating plan.

research_centers.focus: Responsible AI research — bias detection, safety, accountability, governance; Baylor site of the NSF IUCRC on Responsible AI and Governance

research_centers.focus: AI and ML research across LLMs, health AI, smart grid, computational intelligence

computing_infrastructure.summary: Baylor's centralized HPC cluster Kodiak includes ~100 compute nodes, 9 GPU nodes (NVIDIA), one large-memory system and 2.5PB of multi-tiered storage. GPU node count is modest relative to public-R1 peers but meaningful for a private R1; a condominium model lets PIs co-invest in additional capacity.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Bear-Bot 24/7 financial aid access
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Around-the-clock self-service support for financial aid / FAFSA questions reduces access barriers for students whose schedules or language needs don't match business hours.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: CRAIG responsible-AI research agenda
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Baylor's site in CRAIG includes research on bias detection/mitigation and algorithmic accountability intended to make AI safer for affected populations.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Rivas AI Lab research applications
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Pablo Rivas has used machine-learning-and-vision research to train law enforcement to identify signs of human trafficking online — a use of AI aimed at protecting vulnerable people.

regulatory_context.summary: Texas enacted the Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act (TRAIGA / HB 149), signed June 22, 2025 and effective January 1, 2026. It prohibits use of AI for 'restricted purposes' (self-harm, violence, criminality), creates a Texas AI Council, and establishes a regulatory sandbox. SB 1964 separately creates an AI code of ethics for state agencies. In December 2025 a federal executive order signaled potential conflict with state AI laws; Texas legislators publicly opposed federal preemption. Baylor is a private R1, not in a state university system, so it is not governed by a system-level AI policy, but TRAIGA applies broadly to entities doing business in Texas and serving Texas residents.

controversies_and_litigation.summary: Baylor's initial honor-code-based stance on AI writing tools — and its use of AI detection — produced divided opinions among faculty and students, with some faculty taking a more permissive, pedagogy-forward approach. This tension motivated the later University AI Committee and syllabus-level policy model.

narrative_summary: Baylor University's AI posture sits at the intersection of a private R1 research mission, a Christian institutional identity, and a top-down strategic plan ('Baylor in Deeds') that explicitly names the Human-Technology Interface as one of four pillars. Formal AI leadership sits with Provost Nancy Brickhouse, President Linda Livingstone, CIO Jon Allen, and — uniquely — Vice Provost for AI and Institutional Research Gary Carini, a role created August 2025 to pair AI strategy with institutional research. Governance is layered: the 12-member University AI Committee (chaired by Engineering Dean Daniel Pack) owns policy, a broader 18-member Generative AI Working Group feeds sensing and innovation, and an AI Roundtable draws faculty from education, humanities, STEM, law, Honors College, and the Institute for Faith and Learning into monthly philosophical dialogue. Policy posture is cautious but not prohibitive: any new AI tool must be reviewed by ITS and the Provost's Office, sensitive data in non-Baylor-hosted AI is discouraged, and the default Honor Code position treats AI-generated student work as a violation unless the faculty member explicitly opens it via syllabus statement. Enterprise AI deployments include Microsoft 365 Copilot (with the April 2026 licensing change under active re-evaluation), Microsoft Copilot Agents built by the Career Center for resume, interview prep, and career discovery, the Ocelot-powered Bear-Bot financial aid chatbot, and the Primo OneSearch Research Assistant in University Libraries alongside parallel trials of Scopus AI and the Web of Science Research Assistant. Research posture is anchored by Baylor's site in the NSF Industry-University Cooperative Research Center on Responsible AI and Governance (CRAIG) — with Baylor site director Pablo Rivas, partner institutions at Ohio State (lead), Rutgers, and Northeastern, and industry members including Meta, Nationwide, Honda Research, Cisco, Worthington Steel, and Bread Financial — and complemented by the AI and Data Ethics research group in the Baylor Ethics Initiative, the Rivas AI Lab, and the Kodiak HPC cluster (9 GPU nodes, 2.5PB storage). Teaching integration is bottom-up: JPRNM is debuting AI-integrated journalism/PR/new-media courses in AY 2025-26; Hankamer launched what it bills as the nation's first AI-Entrepreneurship Challenge; Graduate Professional Education offers an AI & Ethics series; and the College of Arts & Sciences published a 2025 strategic plan executing the Human-Technology Interface pillar. Texas's TRAIGA law takes effect January 2026; as a private university, Baylor is not subject to a system-level policy but must comply with TRAIGA's restrictions. Key gaps: no AI literacy general-education requirement, no disclosed AI-specific gift or endowment, and the overall enterprise AI deployment remains cautious rather than transformative — consistent with Baylor's value-centered rather than consumption-first framing.
